title: Wiring a solar-powered 12V lighting system for a shed or outbuilding
subtitle: Panel sizing, battery choice, and the 12V circuit that avoids mains wiring
excerpt: A 20 W solar panel, a 20 Ah leisure battery, and a small PWM charge controller give enough capacity to run two 5 W LED lights for 3-4 hours each evening for 4-5 consecutive days without sun. This is a complete off-grid lighting system for a shed, workshop, or outbuilding that requires no mains connection and no Part P notification.
sourceNotes: Solar industry standard guidance on small 12V standalone PV systems. Victron Energy and Renogy documentation on charge controller and battery sizing for small off-grid systems. IET guidance on 12V extra-low voltage systems and Part P exemptions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pulse Width Modulation charge controller. A device that regulates the charging of a battery from a solar panel by rapidly switching the panel connection on and off to control current. Suitable for small 12V systems up to approximately 200 W. Prevents overcharging and extends battery life.

A deep-cycle lead-acid battery designed for repeated partial discharge and recharge, as opposed to a starter battery which is designed for a single high-current burst. Used in campervans, boats, and small off-grid systems. Rated in amp-hours (Ah): a 20 Ah battery stores 20 amps for 1 hour, or 2 amps for 10 hours.

content.text: A 12V system working at extra-low voltage does not require a Part P notification in England, Wales, or Scotland, unlike mains voltage (230V AC) work in outbuildings. This makes a 12V solar lighting system accessible to anyone comfortable with basic electrical connections.
20 W monocrystalline or polycrystalline solar panel (typical: 450 x 650 mm, 12V nominal output)
20 Ah 12V leisure battery (sealed AGM is easier to manage indoors than flooded lead-acid)
(a 10 A controller handles a 20 W panel with significant headroom)
2 x 5 W 12V LED light strips or 12V GU10 LED spotlights
2.5 mm2 twin-core cable from panel to controller; 1.5 mm2 from battery to lights; appropriate 12V fuse holders (5 A inline fuse for the lighting circuit)
Connect the battery to the charge controller first (controller to battery terminals). Most controllers require this sequence to initialise.
Connect the solar panel to the controller (panel terminals). The controller LED should show a charge status.

Connect the lighting circuit to the controller's load output terminals. The load output has low-voltage disconnect: it switches off the lights if the battery drops below approximately 11.5 V, protecting the battery from deep discharge.

Mount the panel on the south-facing roof slope of the shed at the same angle as the roof pitch, or on an adjustable bracket at 30-40°. The cable run from panel to controller should be as short as practical to minimise losses. A
stored indoors or in an insulated box lasts longer than one left in a cold outbuilding in winter: cold significantly reduces effective capacity.
